questions.question: Why does ARP spoofing work on a switched Ethernet segment?
options: ARP replies are authenticated by the switch CPU
ARP is an unauthenticated trust protocol; any host may claim any IP
Switches block gratuitous ARP by default
ARP only travels on wireless media
questions.explanation: RFC 826 ARP carries no signature; receivers cache the most recent IP-MAC pair without verifying ownership.

questions.question: A victim ARP cache flips from the gateway MAC to an unknown MAC every 60 seconds. What is the strongest evidence of an active spoof?
options: The flip frequency exceeds normal DHCP renewal cadence
The unknown MAC also appears as the source of gratuitous ARP replies
questions.explanation: Unsolicited gratuitous ARP from a non-gateway MAC binding the gateway IP is the canonical spoof signature.

questions.question: Which mitigation requires no cooperation from end hosts?
options: Static ARP entries on every client
Dynamic ARP Inspection with a DHCP snooping binding table on the switch
questions.explanation: DAI runs on the switch and validates ARP frames against the DHCP snooping database, protecting all attached hosts transparently.
